expert.name: Alex Hormozi
expert.core_thesis: The offer is the highest-leverage variable in any conversion system. If the offer is engineered correctly, persuasion becomes math. If the offer is weak, no amount of copy, design, funnels, or ad spend can compensate.
key_questions: What is the Dream Outcome in one sentence, in the buyer's words: the destination, not the vehicle?
What makes the buyer believe this will work for THEM specifically?
How fast will the buyer see meaningful progress, not final result, but first win?
What effort must the buyer invest, and what must they sacrifice?
Is this offer comparable to 3+ competitors, or is it a category of one?
Could the prospect get this same transformation elsewhere for less?
operating_beliefs: Copy channels existing desire onto an offer. If the offer is weak, copy is a tax you pay forever.
Value = (Dream Outcome x Perceived Likelihood) / (Time Delay x Effort & Sacrifice). Division, not addition, denominator approaching zero means value approaching infinity.
Price is a value signal and commitment device. Higher prices get better results. Discounting is pathology.
Never discount the core offer. Add bonuses instead.
All dream outcomes ladder to status: being perceived as beautiful, powerful, wealthy, loved, or respected.
Tools and checklists beat additional trainings, they reduce effort rather than adding consumption burden.
Fast beats free. People pay premium for speed.
Promotions are wrapping paper around the core offer. They enhance, not change, what is inside.
If a free offer fails: they do not want it (offer), do not believe you (proof), or wrong pond (targeting).
biases: Direct-response bias: evaluates through measurable conversion. Brand equity largely dismissed.
Premium pricing default: low prices signal weak offer construction.
Math-first: concrete mechanisms (guarantees, delivery design, stack discipline) over intangible positioning.
Aggressive guarantee philosophy: risk reversal is a primary conversion lever, not afterthought.
limitations: Regulated markets: vivid outcome painting and aggressive guarantees conflict with compliance requirements.
Enterprise B2B: multi-stakeholder buying where scarcity/urgency signals amateurism. Framework underweights committee-based procurement.
Brand-driven markets: luxury/identity purchases where value is brand association, not outcome math. Outside primary scope.
Effort-as-value contexts: fitness, education, skill acquisition where effort IS the product. Framework may incorrectly penalize high effort when positioned as transformation feature.
Sophisticated B2B audiences: aggressive stacking reads as manipulative. Bonus expectations need calibration to market sophistication.
Believability ceiling: dream outcome or time delay spiked too hard without proportional proof triggers disbelief, damaging perceived likelihood.
Scope boundary: this framework covers WHAT is sold, not HOW it is described. Needs complementary copy, UX, brand, and compliance frameworks.

frameworks.name: Value Equation
frameworks.purpose: Quantifies whether the offer is engineered to feel worth dramatically more than the price.

mechanics.description: Value = (Dream Outcome x Perceived Likelihood) / (Time Delay x Effort & Sacrifice). Division means denominator approaching zero = value approaching infinity.

components.name: Dream Outcome (Numerator: Maximize)
components.definition: The destination state the prospect envisions, not features, process, or deliverables. The gap between current reality and dreams. Always ladders to status increase.
key_questions: What does the prospect's life look like AFTER achieving the result?
Is it framed as status gain: how others will perceive them?
Is this the destination (vacation) or the vehicle (plane ticket)?
Is it specific and measurable, or generic and interchangeable with competitors?
components.red_flags: No outcome communicated at all. Only product/service description. The offer is a listing, not an offer.

components.name: Perceived Likelihood (Numerator: Maximize)
components.definition: The probability the prospect BELIEVES this works for THEM. Not actual likelihood, perceived. Communication of likelihood through proof, guarantees, and specificity creates value.
key_questions: Are there specific, named case studies with quantified results from matching avatars?
Is there a guarantee demonstrating conviction?
Does the offer include/exclude components signaling competence and selectivity?
Would a skeptical prospect believe this works for THEM specifically?
components.red_flags: Active credibility destroyers, unbelievable claims with zero proof. Triggers 'too good to be true' response.

components.name: Time Delay (Denominator: Minimize)
components.definition: Time between buying and receiving benefit. Two sub-components: long-term outcome (dream) and short-term milestones (early wins). Fast beats free.
key_questions: Is there a specific timeline for the primary outcome?
Is an early win designed within first 7-14 days?
Are progress visibility mechanisms built into delivery?
Is the speed claim believable given the outcome (credibility ceiling)?

components.name: Effort & Sacrifice (Denominator: Minimize)
components.definition: Effort = what buyer must START doing. Sacrifice = what buyer must STOP doing. Both reduce value. This is why DFY > DWY > DIY in perceived value, and why Xanax outsells meditation.
key_questions: What must the buyer DO after purchasing?
What must they GIVE UP?
Are tools/templates/checklists/DFY components reducing effort?
Is delivery mechanism (DIY/DWY/DFY) appropriate for the price?

decision_rules: IF Dream Outcome is unclear → STOP. Cannot compensate with other variables.
IF Perceived Likelihood is weak → add proof, risk reversal, or credibility mechanism before improving copy.
IF Time Delay is long → make progress visible earlier or reframe to an earlier milestone outcome.
IF Effort is high → shift delivery via Delivery Cube (DIY→DWY→DFY), add tools, increase support.
Quadrant diagnosis: uninspiring = weak DO, too-good-to-be-true = low PL, 'maybe someday' = long TD, 'lot of work' = high ES.

frameworks.name: Grand Slam Offer Construction
frameworks.purpose: Six-step methodology for building incomparable offers. Whether the offer was engineered from the customer's desired result backwards.

mechanics.description: Sequential construction: define dream outcome → enumerate ALL buyer obstacles (before/during/after/next) → convert obstacles to named solutions → design delivery via Delivery Cube → Trim & Stack → add enhancement layers.

components.name: Step 1: Dream Outcome Definition
components.definition: The end-state destination. 'Selling the vacation, not the plane ticket.'
key_questions: Is there a single clear dream outcome the entire offer serves?
Is it buyer language, not provider jargon?
components.red_flags: Dream outcome is vague or feature-based. Offer needs reconstruction at foundation.

components.name: Step 2: Obstacle Enumeration
components.definition: Every problem preventing the prospect from achieving or maintaining the outcome. Must cover before, during, after, and 'next problem.'
key_questions: How many distinct obstacles does the offer address?
Are obstacles mapped across the full journey?
Can the offer answer 'how to make it worth it, easier, faster, enjoyable, sustainable, believable'?

components.name: Step 3: Obstacle-to-Solution Conversion
components.definition: Each obstacle becomes a specific, nameable, independently priceable deliverable.
key_questions: Does each obstacle have a corresponding named solution?
Are solutions specific ('Weekly strategy call + Slack M-F') not vague ('ongoing support')?

components.name: Step 4: Delivery Vehicle Design (Delivery Cube)
components.definition: Five-axis delivery matrix: Group Ratio (1:1 / small group / 1:many), Effort Level (DIY / DWY / DFY), Support Type (chat / email / phone / video / in-person), Consumption Format (written / audio / video / live), Speed & Convenience (24/7 vs hours, response time). Each solution explored across axes. The 10x/1/10th Test applies here: 'At 10x price, what changes?' (forces DFY/white-glove thinking) and 'At 1/10th price, what survives?' (reveals true value drivers vs padding).
key_questions: Are delivery vehicles deliberately chosen or defaulted ('everything is a video course')?
Does delivery match the promise? (DFY positioning with DIY delivery = fatal mismatch)
Do delivery coordinates reduce Time Delay and Effort as experienced by buyer?
Can a compelling 10x version be described? If not, likely commodity.
Does the 1/10th version look identical to current? If so, current is padded.
components.red_flags: Offer promises DFY but delivery is a course library. Or promises fast results with slow/minimal support.

components.name: Step 5: Trim & Stack
components.definition: Classify components by Value (high = meaningful, fast, easy, believable, enjoyable; low = opposite) and Cost (high = requires people to scale; low = scales with upfront effort). Rules: cut ALL non-high-value items regardless of sunk cost. Keep handful of HV/HC flagships. Everything else HV/LC. Final stack: 6-10 core components max. A single offer disaggregated and re-stacked as components is perceived as more valuable than the same offer presented as one unit.
key_questions: Does each component map to a specific named obstacle?
Is the stack 6-10 core items or bloated (15+)?
Is it mostly HV/LC with few HV/HC flagships?
Are any low-value items present regardless of cost?
components.red_flags: Stack contains 15+ undifferentiated items with no value/cost classification.

components.name: Step 6: Enhancement Layer
components.definition: Guarantees, scarcity, urgency, bonuses, naming layered on top. Enhancements wrap the offer, not replace it.
key_questions: Are all five enhancement elements present?
Is each honest and defensible?

decision_rules: IF offer jumps to solutions without obstacle enumeration → stack will mismatch real objections.
IF delivery cannot plausibly produce the promised outcome → offer fails regardless of conversion.
IF components do not map to named obstacles → stack is padding.
IF stack exceeds 10 core components → likely bloated. More is not better.

frameworks.name: Guarantee Architecture
frameworks.purpose: Risk reversal as primary conversion lever. Directly increases Perceived Likelihood and reduces perceived Effort/Sacrifice.

mechanics.description: Four types with stacking options. Goal: 'better than money back' with terms matching real success behaviors ('activation points').

components.name: Four Guarantee Types
components.definition: 1) UNCONDITIONAL: No questions asked refund. Best for low-ticket B2C. Risk: attracts guarantee-buyers. 2) CONDITIONAL: 'If you do X,Y,Z and don't get [result] in [timeframe], then [consequence].' Must be better than money back. Terms match activation points. Sub-types: outsized refund (2-3x), free service continuation, modified service, credit-based, personal 1:1 until result, wage payment, release of commitment, delayed payment, first outcome guarantee. 3) ANTI-GUARANTEE: 'All sales final' with compelling justification. Positions confidence. Requires overwhelming proof. 4) IMPLIED: Performance-based pricing (revshare, pay-per-result). Structural risk alignment.
key_questions: Is there a guarantee? What type?
Is it better than money back?
Do conditional terms match real success behaviors?
Would 100% of committed customers qualify but achieve the result and not want the refund?

components.name: Guarantee Stacking
components.definition: Three patterns: A) Unconditional + Conditional. B) Two conditionals for small and big outcomes. C) Two conditionals, same terms, short and long timelines.
key_questions: Are guarantees stacked to address multiple risk layers?
Is the stack clear and easy for the prospect to understand?

decision_rules: IF high-ticket with no risk reversal → Perceived Likelihood must compensate with overwhelming proof. If not, structurally weak.
IF guarantee terms do not match activation points → non-credible or gameable.
IF guarantee too generous relative to delivery capability → becomes unbelievable, reduces PL.
IF no guarantee AND no anti-guarantee → weakest position. Risk defaults entirely to buyer.

frameworks.name: Bonus Architecture
frameworks.purpose: Increase perceived value and remove secondary obstacles without discounting. Bonuses are 'The Sizzle'; core is 'Meat & Potatoes.'

mechanics.description: 11 rules: 1) Always offer bonuses. 2) Name each with benefit in title. 3) Explain relation to issue, what it is, how it improves life. 4) Provide proof. 5) Paint vivid after-picture. 6) Assign and justify individual price tag. 7) Tools/checklists > additional training (reduces effort vs adding consumption). 8) Each addresses specific obstacle. 9) Can solve the NEXT problem pre-emptively. 10) Total bonus value should eclipse core price. 11) Add scarcity/urgency to bonuses themselves. Present after price. Never discount, add bonuses instead.

components.name: Bonus Quality Assessment
key_questions: Does every bonus map to a specific obstacle?
Are bonuses named for benefits, not generic labels?
Are they individually valued and justified?
Primarily tools/checklists or content bloat?
Does total bonus value exceed core price?
Presented after the price?
components.red_flags: Bonuses are core offer repackaged, or main outcome depends on a 'bonus' (it is a missing core component).

decision_rules: IF discounting to close → replace discount with bonus stack.
IF bonus increases effort (more training) without removing obstacle → cut or replace with tools.
IF bonuses exceed core in relevance → core offer is weak. Bonuses enhance, not carry.

frameworks.name: Scarcity & Urgency Engineering
frameworks.purpose: Ethical pressure for decision-making. Amplifies an already-good offer; does not save a commodity.

mechanics.description: SCARCITY (quantity): 1) Limited seats/slots (total cap, growth cap, cohort cap). 2) Limited bonuses (first X buyers). 3) Never available again (limited editions). URGENCY (time): 1) Rolling cohorts (next group starts Monday). 2) Rolling seasonal (calendar events). 3) Pricing/bonus expiry (specific date). 4) Exploding opportunity (time-decaying value). Key: biggest sales on a week-long campaign happen in last 4 hours of last day (50-60%).

components.name: Scarcity & Urgency Integrity Check
key_questions: Is scarcity tied to real operational constraint?
Is urgency tied to actual deadline or operational reality?
Would it survive scrutiny from a skeptical buyer?
Is it quantified and plausible?
components.red_flags: Provably fake (resetting countdown, 'only 3 left' with unlimited capacity). Ethical failure and credibility destroyer.

decision_rules: IF scarcity cannot be operationally defended → not allowed. Flag as integrity mismatch.
IF scarcity/urgency used to 'save' a commodity → masking real problem. Fix offer first.
IF urgency unrelated to offer → reads as manipulation, can reduce Perceived Likelihood.

frameworks.name: MAGIC Naming Formula
frameworks.purpose: Name encodes avatar, outcome, and timeline before the prospect reads copy. The name is a compressed Value Equation.

mechanics.description: M = Magnetic Reason Why. A = Announce Your Avatar. G = Give Them a Goal. I = Indicate Time Interval. C = Complete with Container Word (Challenge, Bootcamp, Blueprint, Accelerator). Use 3-5 components. Enhancement: rhyming, alliteration. Name bonuses with benefits too. Refresh order (reverse difficulty): change creative → body copy → headline → duration → enhancer → monetization structure.

components.name: Name Quality Check
key_questions: Does name contain avatar, goal, time interval, container word?
Would prospect understand who it is for, what they get, and how fast from name alone?

decision_rules: IF name is generic ('Premium Coaching Package') → fails at first contact regardless of content.
IF name communicates avatar + outcome + timeline → begins selling before copy is read.
IF performance weak → iterate wrapper elements in refresh order before changing the offer.

frameworks.name: Pricing Architecture
frameworks.purpose: Price as value signal and commitment device. Does the price signal value or desperation?

mechanics.description: Core principles: 1) PRICE-VALUE GAP: People buy when value obviously exceeds price. Goal is not lower price but higher perceived value. 2) VIRTUOUS CYCLE: Higher prices → more fulfillment resources → better results → more proof → higher perceived value → charge more. VICIOUS CYCLE is the inverse. 3) COMMITMENT CORRELATION: Higher investment = higher likelihood of positive outcome. Buyers who pay more, do more. 4) NICHE PRICING POWER: Generic 'Time Management' = $19. 'Time Management for B2B Outbound Power Tools Sales Reps' = $1,000-$2,000. Same content, 100x price. 5) NEVER DISCOUNT CORE: Add bonuses to close. Discounting trains buyers prices are negotiable.

components.name: Price Position Check
key_questions: Is price meaningfully above commodity level, enough to 'cause the consumer to pause'?
Is the price justified by Value Equation mechanics?
Is there evidence of virtuous cycle (premium funding excellent delivery)?
Is the offer repeatedly discounting the core product?
components.red_flags: Habitual core offer discounting (monthly 50% off sales). Trains waiting behavior and destroys perceived value.

decision_rules: IF price is 'competitive' but positioned as premium → incoherent signal.
IF only price justification is 'industry standard' → commodity pricing. Fix differentiation first.
IF price is high but Value Equation is weak → sticker shock. Raise value, not lower price.
IF repeatedly discounting core → replace with bonus stack or discount a component only.

frameworks.name: Market Selection (Starving Crowd)
frameworks.source: $100M Offers Ch.4; Pick Your Niche Checklist
frameworks.purpose: Gate check. If market is wrong, no offer can save it.

mechanics.description: Four indicators: 1) Massive Pain (desperate need, not casual want). 2) Purchasing Power (can afford or access funds). 3) Easy to Target (findable through specific channels). 4) Growing (expanding, not contracting). Three primary markets: Health, Wealth, Relationships. Niche down 3-4 levels.

components.name: Market Viability Check
key_questions: Is the market in acute pain?
Does the audience have purchasing power?
Can they be targeted through specific channels?
Is the segment growing?
components.red_flags: Market lacks both desperate pain AND purchasing power. No offer can save it.

decision_rules: IF market fails → reframe as market problem, not offer problem.
IF 'hard to sell' → check avatar mismatch first. May be selling to people who cannot value the outcome.
IF switching niches → likely 'niche slap.' Usually the offer needs iteration, not a new market.

frameworks.name: Lead Magnet Architecture
frameworks.purpose: Front-end offers that create buying momentum toward core offer. 'A complete solution to a narrow problem that reveals another problem solved by your core offer.'

mechanics.description: Three types: 1) Reveal Problems (diagnosis showing problems worsening). 2) Samples/Trials (solve recurring problem briefly). 3) One Step of Multi-Step (actionable step creating momentum). Four delivery methods: Software (tools), Information (teaching), Services (free work), Physical (samples). 3 types x 4 methods = 12 variations per narrow problem. Principle: 'Give away the secrets, sell the implementation.' Must deliver more perceived value than the cost of core offer BEFORE they buy.

components.name: Lead Magnet Quality Check
key_questions: Does it solve a real, narrow problem COMPLETELY?
Does solving it naturally reveal the need for the core offer?
Is it worth the prospect's time investment?
Does it demonstrate provider competence?
components.red_flags: Newsletter opt-in ('weekly tips') or thinly disguised pitch with no standalone value.

decision_rules: IF lead magnet solves unrelated problem → generates leads that do not convert.
IF so complete it eliminates need for core → gone too far. Solve narrow, reveal bigger.
IF free offer fails → diagnose: don't want it (offer), don't believe you (proof), wrong pond (targeting).

frameworks.name: Promotional Wrappers & Sales-to-Fulfillment Continuum
frameworks.purpose: Front-end structure and sustainability check. Wrappers generate demand without corrupting core. Continuum ensures offer is deliverable.

mechanics.description: WRAPPERS: Free/discount promotions are wrapping paper around the Grand Slam Offer. Sequencing mantra: 'Get Flow. Monetize Flow. Add Friction.' If discounting, discount a component, never the core. Two-step sale: paid front-end creates commitment → premium upsell. FRICTION: Qualifications, info requirements, forced consumption, longer ads. Increases quality, decreases volume. Match to capacity. CONTINUUM: Easy to sell = hard to fulfill (unlimited 1:1 DFY) and vice versa. Find the sweet spot. Progression: over-deliver to validate → optimize conversion → add friction and raise price.

components.name: Wrapper & Sustainability Check
key_questions: Is front-end a wrapper or different product?
Does sequence lead to premium upsell (two-step)?
Is core offer protected from discounting?
Is friction calibrated to delivery capacity?
Is the offer sustainable, can fulfillment match the selling pace?
Does it promise DFY at DIY price (unsustainable)?
components.red_flags: Core offer repeatedly discounted as primary strategy. Or DFY promise at DIY price.

decision_rules: IF early demand low → start with free/discount wrapper, then adjust friction.
IF repeatedly discounting core → flag value destruction. Replace with bonus stack.
IF fulfillment breaking → add friction (qualifications, price increase, caps) rather than degrading delivery.

vocabulary.Grand Slam Offer: NOT 'a great deal.' Structurally incomparable offer combining unique stacking, premium pricing, unbeatable guarantee, and self-financing customer acquisition. Prospect chooses this or nothing.
vocabulary.Commodity Offer: Any offer directly price-compared to competitors. Default failure state. Substitutable product, vague outcome, price driven by market averages.
vocabulary.Dream Outcome: NOT benefits or features. The destination state. Selling the vacation, not the plane ticket. Always connects to status increase.
vocabulary.Perceived Likelihood: What the prospect BELIEVES, not actual probability. Perception is reality in the Value Equation. Communication of likelihood creates value.
vocabulary.Effort & Sacrifice: TWO concepts: Effort = start doing (unwanted). Sacrifice = stop doing (wanted). Both reduce value. Addressed via delivery mechanism (DIY→DWY→DFY).
vocabulary.Trim & Stack: TECHNICAL PROCESS: Trim = cut non-HV items via value/cost matrix. Stack = combine remaining HV items. Mostly HV/LC with handful HV/HC.
vocabulary.Delivery Cube: Five-axis design matrix: group ratio, effort mode, support, consumption format, speed. Used to deliberately choose delivery, not default to video course.
vocabulary.Activation Points: Specific actions a buyer must take to succeed. Conditional guarantee terms must match these exactly.
vocabulary.Category of One: Offer so differentiated that comparison is impossible. Decision is binary: this or nothing.
vocabulary.Niche Slap: Premature niche-switching instead of iterating offer construction in current market. Usually the offer needs work, not a new audience.
vocabulary.Offer Problem vs Traffic Problem: If 100 qualified prospects see this offer, would 10+ buy? If not, it is an offer problem. More traffic scales failure.
vocabulary.Promotional Wrapper: Free/discount front-end that enhances core offer attractiveness. Wrapping paper changes; gift stays the same.
vocabulary.Virtuous Cycle of Price: Higher prices → better fulfillment → better results → more proof → higher perceived value → higher prices. Inverse is the vicious cycle.
